_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for St. Landry Parish, Louisiana. Record survival, courthouse history, and known losses for the mother parish of western Acadiana. St. Landry is one of the original Louisiana parishes (created 1807), and its courthouse at Opelousas holds colonial-era records spanning the French, Spanish, and early American periods. The Opelousas Post was the administrative center of the western Attakapas-Opelousas district, and St. Landry's records are the gateway to all of western Acadiana genealogy.
display_name: St. Landry Parish, Louisiana -- Courthouse Atlas

current_courthouse.location: Opelousas, LA -- 118 South Court Street. WPA-era courthouse in the center of Opelousas.

previous_courthouses.location: Opelousas -- early frame courthouse. Opelousas had served as the Opelousas Post administrative center since the 1720s under French and then Spanish rule.
Opelousas -- brick courthouse, one of the substantial early Louisiana courthouses
previous_courthouses.fate: Severely damaged in the Opelousas courthouse fire of 1876.

previous_courthouses.location: Opelousas -- rebuilt courthouse on the same site after the 1876 fire
previous_courthouses.fate: Replaced by the current WPA-era courthouse. Records transferred.

records_destroyed: some loose papers and civil suit files
records_survived: colonial-era documents (most survived, having been stored separately)
record_losses.description: The 1876 courthouse fire at Opelousas was the most significant record loss event in western Acadiana. However, the damage was less catastrophic than fires at many other Louisiana courthouses. Most bound record books survived because they were in fireproof storage or were rescued during the fire. The colonial-era records -- French and Spanish documents from the Opelousas Post -- largely survived because they had been stored separately. Loose papers, some court minutes, and portions of the notarial archive were lost. The parish rebuilt on the same site and the surviving records were rehoused.
record_losses.narrative_impact: The 1876 fire damaged but did not destroy St. Landry's extraordinary record collection. If {ancestor_name}'s family was at the Opelousas Post in the colonial era, their records likely survived -- but some court proceedings and notarial acts from the mid-nineteenth century may be among the losses.
record_losses.source: Brasseaux, Carl A. The Opelousas Post: A History. Lafayette: Center for Louisiana Studies, 1987.

record_losses.cause: Daughter parish creation -- Acadia (1886), Evangeline (1910), Allen (1912, partially from St. Landry), and Beauregard (1912, partially from St. Landry) carved from St. Landry
records_survived: Pre-creation records for all daughter parish areas remain at St. Landry
record_losses.description: St. Landry was the mother parish for much of western Louisiana. Its daughter parishes' pre-creation records remain at Opelousas. Researchers working in Acadia, Evangeline, and parts of Allen and Beauregard parishes must begin at the St. Landry courthouse for earlier records. This makes St. Landry one of the most important courthouses in Louisiana for genealogical research.
record_losses.narrative_impact: St. Landry is the mother of western Louisiana parishes -- if {ancestor_name}'s family lived anywhere in the prairie region before 1886-1912, the search begins at Opelousas.
record_losses.source: Hebert, Donald J. Southwest Louisiana Records. Eunice, LA: self-published, 1974-2000.

deeds.completeness: Good -- conveyance records are continuous from the early American period, with some colonial-era land grants recorded in the earliest volumes. French and Spanish language documents appear through the 1830s.
deeds.gaps: The 1876 fire damaged some volumes but most conveyance books survived. Colonial-era land grants are supplemented by records at the Louisiana State Archives.
deeds.repository: St. Landry Parish Clerk of Court, Opelousas
deeds.online_access: Limited -- some indexes through Louisiana Secretary of State

wills_estates.completeness: Good -- succession records from early statehood. The succession records are among the most genealogically important in Louisiana because St. Landry served as the mother parish for a vast territory.
wills_estates.gaps: Some loss in the 1876 fire, primarily loose papers. Bound succession record books largely survived. Colonial-era successions supplemented by Louisiana State Archives.
wills_estates.repository: St. Landry Parish Clerk of Court, Opelousas

marriages.completeness: Good from early statehood. Catholic sacramental records at St. Landry Church in Opelousas (established 1776) are significantly earlier.
marriages.gaps: Some early marriage records damaged in the 1876 fire. Church records fill most gaps.
marriages.repository: St. Landry Parish Clerk of Court; Diocese of Lafayette archives
marriages.online_access: Some indexes at Louisiana Secretary of State

court_minutes.completeness: Fair -- some early court minutes were lost in the 1876 fire. Post-1876 records are continuous.
court_minutes.gaps: Pre-1876 court minutes are incomplete. Police Jury records documenting cattle brands, road construction, and parish governance partially survive.
court_minutes.repository: St. Landry Parish Clerk of Court, Opelousas

vital_records.completeness: Fair -- compliance was slow in rural areas. Catholic church records are more reliable for the early period, particularly for the gens de couleur libres community.
vital_records.repository: Louisiana Office of Vital Records, New Orleans

substitute_sources.source_type: Colonial records -- French and Spanish Opelousas Post
substitute_sources.description: The Opelousas Post records from the French (pre-1762) and Spanish (1762-1803) colonial periods include land grants, censuses, church records, commandant records, and notarial acts. Some survive at the St. Landry courthouse; others are at the Louisiana State Archives and in Spanish archives.
substitute_sources.repository: St. Landry Parish Clerk of Court; Louisiana State Archives, Baton Rouge; Archivo General de Indias (Seville, Spain, via microfilm)
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: The colonial records are the foundational documents for all western Acadiana genealogy. They document the original Acadian settlement (1765 onward), the free Creole of Color community, and the mixed French-Spanish-Native population of the Opelousas district.
substitute_sources.source: Brasseaux, Carl A. The Opelousas Post: A History. Lafayette: Center for Louisiana Studies, 1987.

substitute_sources.source_type: Catholic parish sacramental records -- St. Landry Church, Opelousas
substitute_sources.description: St. Landry Church in Opelousas (established 1776) maintains sacramental registers from its founding. These are among the oldest church records in western Louisiana and document baptisms, marriages, and burials across the Opelousas district before daughter parishes were established.
substitute_sources.repository: St. Landry Church, Opelousas; Diocese of Lafayette archives
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: The church records at St. Landry predate civil records and document all segments of the population -- white Acadians and Creoles, gens de couleur libres, and enslaved people (especially baptisms). The registers are in French and Latin.
substitute_sources.source: Hebert, Donald J. Southwest Louisiana Records. Eunice, LA: self-published, 1974-2000.

substitute_sources.description: St. Landry Parish had one of the largest free Creole of Color communities in the South. Notarial archives, church records, and civil records document this community from the colonial period through emancipation. The Opelousas-area gens de couleur libres owned property, held enslaved people in some cases, and maintained a distinct social identity.
substitute_sources.repository: St. Landry Parish Clerk of Court; Diocese of Lafayette; Louisiana State Archives
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential for researchers tracing free Creole of Color families. The notarial archives contain emancipation acts, property transactions, and marriage contracts that document this community's legal and economic life. These records survived the 1876 fire in sufficient quantity to support research.
substitute_sources.source: Mills, Gary B. The Forgotten People: Cane River's Creoles of Color. Baton Rouge: LSU Press, 1977.

substitute_sources.description: Donald J. Hebert's multi-volume compilation is the essential published source for St. Landry Parish genealogy, abstracting church and civil records across western Acadiana with St. Landry as the central focus.
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: The single most important published genealogical resource for St. Landry Parish. Hebert cross-references families across parishes and decades, making it possible to trace Acadian and Creole family networks across the entire region.
substitute_sources.source: Hebert, Donald J. Southwest Louisiana Records. 39 vols. Eunice, LA: self-published, 1974-2000.

narrative_hooks: St. Landry is the mother parish of western Louisiana -- {ancestor_name}'s family records at Opelousas may stretch from the colonial era through the creation of daughter parishes, documenting generations in a single courthouse vault.

The 1876 courthouse fire at Opelousas damaged but did not destroy the records -- most bound volumes survived, including the colonial-era documents that trace {ancestor_name}'s family back to the Opelousas Post.

If {ancestor_name} was part of the gens de couleur libres community at Opelousas, the notarial archives document a free Creole of Color family that owned property, made contracts, and navigated a three-tier racial system unique to Louisiana.

The church records at St. Landry in Opelousas predate the civil records by decades -- if {ancestor_name}'s baptism was recorded in 1780, it was in French, in Latin, and in a church where Acadian exile families gathered to rebuild their community.
source_references: Hebert, Donald J. Southwest Louisiana Records. 39 vols. Eunice, LA: self-published, 1974-2000.
Brasseaux, Carl A. The Opelousas Post: A History. Lafayette: Center for Louisiana Studies, 1987.
Mills, Gary B. The Forgotten People: Cane River's Creoles of Color. Baton Rouge: LSU Press, 1977.
Brasseaux, Carl A. The Founding of New Acadia. Baton Rouge: LSU Press, 1987.
Winters, John D. The Civil War in Louisiana. Baton Rouge: LSU Press, 1963.
